ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general black tea beverage class; no specific peach-flavoured or less-sugar variant exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR covers ready-to-drink black tea; no specific peach-flavoured less-sugar subcode exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest defensible parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for peach-flavoured less-sugar black tea beverage found in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to a regular-sized cup as commonly sold at Singaporean bubble tea and speciality tea outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Peach Black Tea (Less Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Peach Black Tea (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010085, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened beverages and tea-based drinks — analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened tea
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for peach black tea (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney scale) based on the closest analogous entry: sucrose-sweetened tea beverages with comparable sugar content (~8g/100g). Pure sucrose has a GI of 65; dilution in a tea matrix and the modest sugar concentration support a slightly lower estimate of 55, which falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 20.65) / 100 ≈ 11.4, using a 250 g serving with 8.26 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.26 g sugar per 100 g; liquid sugar independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Despite the 'less sugar' label, this beverage contains 8.26 g sugar per 100 g and qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB); regular SSB consumption is independently associated with elevated blood pressure per AHA 2018 guidelines, and individuals with hypertension should request a genuinely unsweetened (zero-sugar) version or choose plain brewed tea.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.26 g sugar per 100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g); rapid glycaemic impact from liquid sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage remains a sugar-sweetened drink with 8.26 g sugar per 100 g and virtually no fibre to blunt glycaemic response; per ADA guidelines, 'less sugar' variants of SSBs are not considered safe for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, and a genuinely unsweetened (sugar = 0 g) version should be requested instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar at 8.26 g per 100 g contributes to excess caloric intake, triglyceride elevation, and cardiometabolic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages including reduced-sugar variants is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage and prefer plain unsweetened black tea, which provides polyphenols without added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 8.26 g per 100 g with negligible fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The residual sugar content (8.26 g/100 g) with near-zero fibre continues to support a pro-inflammatory environment; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to genuinely unsweetened peach black tea to access theaflavin anti-inflammatory benefits without the pro-inflammatory effect of added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peach Black Tea (Less Sugar) is a freshly brewed black tea beverage flavoured with peach, prepared with a reduced quantity of added sugar compared to the standard sweetened version. It is widely available at Singaporean speciality tea and bubble tea outlets, where customers can further customise the sugar level and add toppings such as pearls or jelly. The drink has a light amber colour, a mild astringency from the black tea base, and a sweet-fruity peach aroma. Per 100 g it provides 43 kcal, 8.26 g carbohydrate (all from sugar), 0.01 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 0.03 g dietary fibre, and 1 mg sodium. Despite the 'less sugar' designation, the beverage still contains 8.26 g sugar per 100 g, qualifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under ADA, AHA, and HPB Singapore definitions. This means it is not suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension without further sugar reduction to zero. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI category per University of Sydney scale) based on analogous sucrose-sweetened tea data from the University of Sydney GI Database, yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 11.4 per 250 ml serving. Black tea polyphenols (theaflavins and thearubigins) are present but cannot offset the glycaemic impact of the residual sugar in this formulation. For clinical populations, a genuinely unsweetened version is recommended.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact product (cr_id D08010085) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source (D08010085); this is consistent with a brewed tea beverage containing no fat-bearing ingredients. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same source and food type. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this beverage. GI is an analogous estimate (55, low — GI ≤55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney three-tier scale) based on sucrose-sweetened tea data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found. Glycaemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 20.65) / 100 ≈ 11.4. Note: although GI 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category, it is correctly classified as low per the University of Sydney standard (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2 per G5 hard gate requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index of 55. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale, GI ≤55 is low, GI 56–69 is medium, GI ≥70 is high. A GI of exactly 55 falls in the low category. Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification boundary explicitly.
